name: Palace of Versailles
summary: The Palace of Versailles is a former royal residence commissioned by King Louis XIV located in Versailles, about 12 miles southwest of Paris. Originally a hunting lodge built by his father Louis XIII, it was transformed into the largest and most opulent palace in Europe, serving as the seat of French political power from 1682 until the French Revolution in 1789. Today it is one of the most visited monuments in France and a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

timeline.title: Louis XIV Begins Expansion
timeline.description: After the death of Cardinal Mazarin, Louis XIV begins his personal rule and orders architect Louis Le Vau to transform his father's modest hunting lodge at Versailles into a grand palace. The Sun King envisions a residence that will project his absolute power and centralize the French aristocracy under his watchful eye.

timeline.title: Court Moves to Versailles
timeline.description: Louis XIV officially moves the French court and government from Paris to Versailles, establishing it as the seat of political power. The palace becomes home to approximately 3,000 to 10,000 courtiers, servants, and officials, all living under the strict etiquette system designed to control the nobility through ritual and proximity to the king.

timeline.title: Completion of the Chapel
timeline.description: The Royal Chapel of Versailles, the final major structure added under Louis XIV, is completed by architect Robert de Cotte. This magnificent two-story chapel features a painted ceiling by Antoine Coypel and becomes one of the finest examples of French Baroque architecture. Louis XIV's building program is now essentially complete.

timeline.title: French Revolution Forces Royal Return
timeline.description: On October 6, 1789, a mob of thousands of Parisian women, angry over bread shortages, marches to Versailles and forces King Louis XVI and Marie Antoinette to return to Paris. The royal family never returns to Versailles, effectively ending the palace's 107-year role as the seat of French political power.

timeline.title: Louis-Philippe Creates Museum
timeline.description: King Louis-Philippe transforms the Palace of Versailles into the Museum of the History of France, dedicated to all the glories of France. Rather than restoring the royal apartments, he fills the palace's grand galleries with paintings and sculptures depicting major events in French history, preserving the building while redefining its purpose.

timeline.title: Treaty of Versailles Signed
timeline.description: The Treaty of Versailles, officially ending World War I, is signed in the Hall of Mirrors on June 28, 1919. The choice of this location is deliberately symbolic: it was in the same hall that the German Empire had been proclaimed in 1871 after defeating France. The treaty imposes heavy reparations on Germany and reshapes the map of Europe.

timeline.title: Grand Versailles Restoration Project
timeline.description: France launches the ambitious Grand Versailles restoration project, a 17-year plan to restore the palace and its gardens to their original 18th-century splendor. The project includes the restoration of the Hall of Mirrors, the royal apartments, and the fountains, many of which are returned to their original working condition.

images.caption: The Palace of Versailles at the height of Louis XIV's grand expansion, with its gleaming facades and formal gardens
The October March as Parisian women storm Versailles and force the royal family to return to Paris
The signing of the Treaty of Versailles in the Hall of Mirrors, ending World War I
The fully restored Palace of Versailles with its magnificent gardens stretching to the horizon
